content.text: How Policy Inheritance Works

Azure Policy assignments cascade downward through the scope hierarchy. A policy assigned at a management group automatically applies to every subscription, resource group, and resource beneath that management group — without any additional assignments being required. This is called policy inheritance.

Inheritance is one-way and cannot be blocked by child scopes. A subscription Owner cannot remove or override a policy assigned at a parent management group. The only way to change an inherited policy's effect is for a principal with write permission at the MG scope to modify or remove the assignment at its source.

attrs.alt: Policy inheritance diagram: policies flow down from Root MG through Corp MG and Corp-Prod MG to subscription level

content.text: The Effective Policy Set

Every resource in Azure is subject to an effective policy set — the union of all policy assignments that apply to it from all scope levels. When Azure Policy evaluates a resource (at deployment time for deny effects, or during compliance scans for audit effects), it collects assignments from:
The root management group (tenant scope)
Every management group in the hierarchy between root and the subscription
The subscription itself
The resource group the resource belongs to

The policy engine evaluates all collected assignments against the resource. If any deny-effect assignment triggers, the deployment is rejected — regardless of whether the deny came from a high-level MG or a direct subscription assignment.

# List the effective policy set for a specific subscription
# (includes policies inherited from all parent MGs)
SUB='11111111-1111-1111-1111-111111111111'

# Method 1: list all policy states (already-evaluated compliance)
az policy state list \
  --subscription $SUB \
  --query '[].{Policy:policyDefinitionName,Resource:resourceId,State:complianceState}' \
  -o table

# Method 2: list all policy assignments that apply to the subscription
# This includes assignments at RG, sub, MG, and root scopes
az policy assignment list \
  --subscription $SUB \
  --disable-scope-strict-match \
  --query '[].{Name:displayName,Scope:scope,Effect:parameters.effect.value}' \
  -o table

# The --disable-scope-strict-match flag includes inherited assignments
# from parent management groups, not just direct assignments at sub scope
Policy Effect Priority

When multiple policies apply to the same resource, the policy engine does not have a simple 'last wins' or 'first wins' rule. Instead, each policy assignment is evaluated independently. A resource must satisfy ALL applicable policy assignments. A single deny blocks the deployment regardless of how many other assignments allow it.
However, the effects themselves do have an evaluation order within a single policy evaluation cycle:
Evaluation order
Effect
What it does
Disabled
Policy is turned off — no evaluation
Append
Adds fields to the resource before it is created or updated
Modify
Adds, updates, or removes tags or properties on the resource
Deny
Blocks the deployment — ARM returns an error
Audit
Allows the deployment but marks the resource as non-compliant
AuditIfNotExists
Audits a related resource that should exist but doesn't
DeployIfNotExists
Deploys a related resource if it doesn't exist

Deny effects are checked before Audit effects in a single policy evaluation, but across multiple assignments, each policy is evaluated independently. A resource blocked by one deny assignment is blocked even if another audit assignment on the same resource would have passed.
Exemptions: Excluding Resources From Inherited Policy

When an inherited policy is correct for the overall scope but needs to be relaxed for a specific resource or resource group, Azure Policy exemptions provide the mechanism. An exemption does not modify the policy assignment — it marks a specific resource scope as exempt from a specific assignment.
There are two exemption categories:
Waiver: the resource is genuinely exempt from the policy requirement (e.g., a legacy system that cannot be changed)
Mitigated: the resource satisfies the policy intent through a different mechanism (e.g., a resource in a secure network does not need a specific tag because the network controls access)

# Create an exemption for a specific resource group from an inherited policy
# Scenario: rg-legacy contains an old storage account that cannot add the required tag

POLICY_ASSIGNMENT_ID=$(az policy assignment show \
  --name 'require-env-tag' \
  --scope '/providers/Microsoft.Management/managementGroups/mg-corp' \
  --query id -o tsv)

RG_SCOPE='/subscriptions/11111111-1111-1111-1111-111111111111/resourceGroups/rg-legacy'

az policy exemption create \
  --name 'legacy-rg-tag-waiver' \
  --display-name 'Legacy RG: waiver from require-env-tag until migration' \
  --policy-assignment $POLICY_ASSIGNMENT_ID \
  --scope $RG_SCOPE \
  --exemption-category Waiver \
  --expires-on '2026-12-31T00:00:00Z' \
  --description 'rg-legacy contains pre-2024 resources that cannot be tagged; scheduled for migration in Q4 2026'

# List all exemptions in a subscription
az policy exemption list \
  --subscription 11111111-1111-1111-1111-111111111111 \
  --query '[].{Name:name,Scope:scope,Category:exemptionCategory,Expires:expiresOn}' \
  -o table
Policy Initiatives: Grouping Assignments

A policy initiative (also called a policy set definition) groups multiple individual policies into a single assignment. Assigning one initiative at a management group is equivalent to assigning every policy in the initiative at that scope — all members inherit together. Initiatives reduce assignment count and make the governance intent readable.

# Create a policy initiative that bundles tagging, region, and security policies
az policy set-definition create \
  --name 'corp-baseline' \
  --display-name 'Corp Baseline Governance Initiative' \
  --definitions '[
    {"policyDefinitionId":"/providers/Microsoft.Authorization/policyDefinitions/1e30110a-5ceb-460c-a204-c1c3969c6d62","parameters":{"tagName":{"value":"environment"}}},
    {"policyDefinitionId":"/providers/Microsoft.Authorization/policyDefinitions/e56962a6-4747-49cd-b67b-bf8b01975c4f","parameters":{"listOfAllowedLocations":{"value":["eastus","eastus2","westeurope"]}}},
    {"policyDefinitionId":"/providers/Microsoft.Authorization/policyDefinitions/4fa4b6c0-31ca-4c0d-b10d-24b96f62a751"}
  ]'

# Assign the initiative at Corp MG — all three policies inherit to all Corp subscriptions
az policy assignment create \
  --name 'corp-baseline-assignment' \
  --display-name 'Corp Baseline Governance' \
  --policy-set-definition 'corp-baseline' \
  --scope '/providers/Microsoft.Management/managementGroups/mg-corp'

# List all initiatives assigned at a management group
az policy assignment list \
  --scope '/providers/Microsoft.Management/managementGroups/mg-corp' \
  --query '[?policyDefinitionId contains "policySetDefinitions"].{Name:displayName,Initiative:policyDefinitionId}' \
  -o table
Compliance State and Remediation

The Azure Policy compliance engine evaluates resources on a regular schedule (roughly every 24 hours) and marks each resource as compliant or non-compliant against every applicable policy. For audit-effect policies, non-compliance is recorded but the resource is not changed. For deployIfNotExists and modify effects, remediation tasks can be triggered to bring non-compliant resources into compliance.

# Check compliance state across all resources in a subscription
az policy state summarize \
  --subscription 11111111-1111-1111-1111-111111111111 \
  --query '{
    TotalNonCompliant: results.policyStates.noncompliantCount,
    TotalCompliant: results.policyStates.compliantCount
  }' -o json

# List non-compliant resources for a specific policy assignment
az policy state list \
  --subscription 11111111-1111-1111-1111-111111111111 \
  --filter "complianceState eq 'NonCompliant' and policyAssignmentName eq 'require-env-tag'" \
  --query '[].{Resource:resourceId,Policy:policyDefinitionName}' \
  -o table

# Create a remediation task for a deployIfNotExists policy
# (e.g., deploy diagnostic settings to storage accounts that don't have them)
az policy remediation create \
  --name 'remediate-storage-diag' \
  --policy-assignment '/subscriptions/11111111-1111-1111-1111-111111111111/providers/Microsoft.Authorization/policyAssignments/deploy-storage-diagnostics' \
  --resource-discovery-mode ReEvaluateCompliance \
  --subscription 11111111-1111-1111-1111-111111111111

# Monitor remediation task status
az policy remediation show \
  --name 'remediate-storage-diag' \
  --subscription 11111111-1111-1111-1111-111111111111 \
  --query '{Status:provisioningState,Succeeded:deploymentSummary.successfulDeployments,Failed:deploymentSummary.failedDeployments}' \
  -o json
Terraform: Managing Policy Inheritance

Terraform's `azurerm` provider manages policy assignments at each scope level through distinct resource types. Assignments at management group scope use `azurerm_management_group_policy_assignment`; at subscription scope, `azurerm_subscription_policy_assignment`; at resource group scope, `azurerm_resource_group_policy_assignment`. Exemptions use `azurerm_management_group_policy_exemption` or the subscription/RG equivalents.
KQL: Querying Policy Compliance Across Inherited Policies

The policy state data is available in Azure Resource Graph as the `PolicyResources` table. This allows cross-subscription compliance queries that reveal which inherited policies are causing non-compliance and where.

// 1. Non-compliant resources grouped by policy name and subscription
PolicyResources
| where type == 'microsoft.policyinsights/policystates'
| where properties.complianceState == 'NonCompliant'
| extend policyName = tostring(properties.policyDefinitionName)
| extend subId = tostring(properties.subscriptionId)
| summarize nonCompliantCount = count() by policyName, subId
| join kind=leftouter (
    ResourceContainers
    | where type == 'microsoft.resources/subscriptions'
    | project subId = subscriptionId, subName = name
) on subId
| project subName, policyName, nonCompliantCount
| order by nonCompliantCount desc

// 2. Resources exempted from policies — audit the exemption list
PolicyResources
| where type == 'microsoft.policyinsights/policystates'
| where properties.complianceState == 'Exempt'
| project resourceId = id, policyName = tostring(properties.policyDefinitionName),
          exemptionId = tostring(properties.policyExemptionIds[0])
| order by policyName asc

// 3. Which management group policies are causing the most non-compliance?
PolicyResources
| where type == 'microsoft.policyinsights/policystates'
| where properties.complianceState == 'NonCompliant'
| extend policyScope = tostring(properties.policyAssignmentScope)
| where policyScope startswith '/providers/Microsoft.Management/'
| extend mgName = extract(@'managementGroups/([^/]+)', 1, policyScope)
| summarize count() by mgName, policyName = tostring(properties.policyDefinitionName)
| order by count_ desc
Common Policy Inheritance Mistakes
Mistake
Consequence
Prevention
Assigning deny policies at Root MG without testing
Breaks deployments in all subscriptions including platform subscriptions
Test in audit mode first; use exclusions for Platform MG before enabling deny at root
Assuming a subscription Owner can remove inherited policies
Governance assumption violated; teams attempt removal and create support tickets
Document that MG policies are immutable from subscription scope; communicate at vending time
Not setting exemption expiry dates
Permanent exemptions accumulate; audit shows large non-compliant exemption set
Require expiry on all exemptions; use policy to audit exemptions without expiry
Creating one policy assignment per subscription instead of using MG inheritance
N subscriptions = N assignments to maintain; drift between subscriptions
Assign once at MG; let inheritance propagate — add subscription-specific overrides only where truly needed
Not creating remediation tasks after deployIfNotExists assignment
Existing non-compliant resources remain so forever; only new resources get the fix
After any deployIfNotExists assignment, immediately create a remediation task for the existing resource set
Testing Inherited Policy Impact Before Moving a Subscription

Before moving a subscription into a management group with an existing policy set, it is essential to evaluate the impact in audit mode first. A deny policy that is correct for the target MG may block resources already deployed in the subscription. The following process uses a temporary audit-mode assignment to discover non-compliant resources before the move.

#!/usr/bin/env bash
# pre-move-policy-check.sh
# Usage: ./pre-move-policy-check.sh <subscription-id> <target-mg-id>

SUB_ID=$1
TARGET_MG=$2
TEMP_ASSIGNMENT_PREFIX='pre-move-test'

echo "=== Step 1: List all policies at target MG ==="
az policy assignment list \
  --scope "/providers/Microsoft.Management/managementGroups/$TARGET_MG" \
  --query '[].{Name:name,DisplayName:displayName,Effect:parameters.effect.value}' \
  -o table

echo ''
echo '=== Step 2: Create temporary AUDIT-mode copies on the subscription ==='
# Copy each deny policy as an audit assignment to preview impact
DENY_POLICIES=$(az policy assignment list \
  --scope "/providers/Microsoft.Management/managementGroups/$TARGET_MG" \
  --query '[?parameters.effect.value==`Deny`].{name:name,defId:policyDefinitionId}' \
  -o json)

echo $DENY_POLICIES | jq -c '.[]' | while read policy; do
  PNAME=$(echo $policy | jq -r '.name')
  DEFID=$(echo $policy | jq -r '.defId')
  az policy assignment create \
    --name "${TEMP_ASSIGNMENT_PREFIX}-${PNAME}" \
    --policy $DEFID \
    --scope "/subscriptions/$SUB_ID" \
    --enforcement-mode DoNotEnforce
  echo "Created audit copy: ${TEMP_ASSIGNMENT_PREFIX}-${PNAME}"
done

echo ''
echo 'Waiting 120s for compliance scan...'
sleep 120

echo ''
echo '=== Step 3: Check non-compliant resources ==='
az policy state list \
  --subscription $SUB_ID \
  --filter "complianceState eq 'NonCompliant' and policyAssignmentName startswith '${TEMP_ASSIGNMENT_PREFIX}'" \
  --query '[].{Resource:resourceId,Policy:policyDefinitionName}' \
  -o table

echo ''
echo '=== Step 4: Clean up temp assignments ==='
az policy assignment list \
  --subscription $SUB_ID \
  --query "[?starts_with(name,'${TEMP_ASSIGNMENT_PREFIX}')].name" \
  -o tsv | while read name; do
    az policy assignment delete --name $name --scope "/subscriptions/$SUB_ID"
    echo "Deleted: $name"
  done

Run this script at least 24 hours before the planned MG move. Fix non-compliant resources before moving the subscription — attempting to remediate after the move exposes the team to denied deployments during the remediation window.
DeployIfNotExists Policies: Automatic Compliance at Scale

DeployIfNotExists (DINE) policies automatically deploy a related resource when the trigger resource does not have the required companion. The most common example is deploying diagnostic settings to a storage account or key vault automatically when one is created, without requiring the deployment team to remember to add them.

# Deploy the DINE policy and assign it at Corp MG
# The managed identity created for the assignment needs Contributor on the subscription

# Create the policy definition
az policy definition create \
  --name 'deploy-storage-diagnostics' \
  --display-name 'Deploy diagnostic settings for Storage Accounts' \
  --mode All \
  --rules '@storage-diag-policy.json'

# Assign at Corp MG with a managed identity for the deployment
az policy assignment create \
  --name 'storage-diag-deploy' \
  --display-name 'Auto-deploy Storage Diagnostics' \
  --policy 'deploy-storage-diagnostics' \
  --scope '/providers/Microsoft.Management/managementGroups/mg-corp' \
  --location eastus \
  --mi-system-assigned

# Grant the assignment's managed identity Contributor on all Corp subscriptions
# (needed to deploy diagnostic settings resources)
ASSIGNMENT_PRINCIPAL=$(az policy assignment show \
  --name 'storage-diag-deploy' \
  --scope '/providers/Microsoft.Management/managementGroups/mg-corp' \
  --query 'identity.principalId' -o tsv)

az role assignment create \
  --assignee-object-id $ASSIGNMENT_PRINCIPAL \
  --role Contributor \
  --scope '/providers/Microsoft.Management/managementGroups/mg-corp'
Policy Assignment Parameters and Overrides

When the same policy is assigned at multiple levels with different parameters, the lower-scope assignment's parameters take effect for that scope. This is not conflict resolution — both assignments exist independently. Each triggers its own evaluation with its own parameters.

# Example: Allowed regions policy assigned at two levels with different region lists

# Corp MG: strict regions
az policy assignment create \
  --name 'corp-allowed-regions' \
  --policy 'e56962a6-4747-49cd-b67b-bf8b01975c4f' \
  --scope '/providers/Microsoft.Management/managementGroups/mg-corp' \
  --params '{"listOfAllowedLocations":{"value":["eastus","eastus2","westeurope"]}}'

# A specific subscription needs australiaeast as well
# Assign ANOTHER instance of the same policy at subscription scope
# (not an override — both assignments apply to this subscription)
# This creates a MORE restrictive effective set, not a less restrictive one:
# a resource in australiaeast must satisfy BOTH assignments,
# and it fails the Corp MG assignment even if it passes the subscription one.

# CORRECT approach: use an exemption at subscription scope to exempt from
# the Corp MG assignment, then assign the broader list at subscription scope

# Step 1: Exempt the sub from the Corp MG allowed-regions assignment
CORP_ASSIGNMENT_ID=$(az policy assignment show \
  --name 'corp-allowed-regions' \
  --scope '/providers/Microsoft.Management/managementGroups/mg-corp' \
  --query id -o tsv)

az policy exemption create \
  --name 'apac-sub-region-exemption' \
  --policy-assignment $CORP_ASSIGNMENT_ID \
  --scope '/subscriptions/22222222-2222-2222-2222-222222222222' \
  --exemption-category Mitigated \
  --description 'APAC sub uses australiaeast; mitigated by sub-level assignment'

# Step 2: Assign the broader region list at subscription scope
az policy assignment create \
  --name 'apac-allowed-regions' \
  --policy 'e56962a6-4747-49cd-b67b-bf8b01975c4f' \
  --scope '/subscriptions/22222222-2222-2222-2222-222222222222' \
  --params '{"listOfAllowedLocations":{"value":["eastus","eastus2","westeurope","australiaeast"]}}'
Summary

Azure Policy inheritance cascades policy assignments downward from management groups to every subscription, resource group, and resource beneath them. The effective policy set for any resource is the union of all assignments from root management group through each parent MG, the subscription, and the resource group. Deny effects from any level in this chain block deployment regardless of other policies. Exemptions allow scoped exceptions without modifying the original assignment. Policy initiatives group multiple policies into single assignments, reducing management overhead while maintaining full inheritance. The platform team's ability to govern through MG-level policy — with subscription Owners unable to override inherited assignments — is the technical foundation of secure subscription democratization.
